title: PBR in Shaders
description: Physically-based rendering (PBR) replaces Blinn-Phong's empirical heuristics with an energy-conserving BRDF grounded in microfacet theory. The Cook-Torrance model decomposes specular reflection into three terms: the Fresnel equation (F), the geometry function (G), and the normal distribution function (D). This lesson builds each term from scratch on a sphere.

theory.key_concept: The Cook-Torrance specular BRDF is: `f_r = (D * F * G) / (4 * dot(N,L) * dot(N,V))`. It is evaluated per-fragment and produces energy-conserving specular reflections. The `roughness` parameter drives D and G; `metallic` drives how much Fresnel governs the base reflectance F0.
theory.fresnel: Schlick's Fresnel approximation: `F = F0 + (1-F0) * pow(1 - dot(H,V), 5)`. F0 is the base reflectance at 0° incidence — ~0.04 for dielectrics (plastic, skin), ~0.95 for metals. Fresnel makes surfaces more reflective at grazing angles, which is why every surface looks more mirror-like at the edges.
theory.ndf: The GGX Normal Distribution Function (NDF): `D = alpha^2 / (PI * (NdotH^2 * (alpha^2 - 1) + 1)^2)` where `alpha = roughness^2`. It describes how microfacets are distributed around the half-vector. Rough surfaces have a wide lobe; smooth surfaces concentrate it to a sharp peak.
theory.geometry: The Smith geometry function combined with GGX geometry shadowing: `G = G1(N,L) * G1(N,V)` where `G1(n,x) = NdotX / (NdotX * (1-k) + k)` and `k = (roughness+1)^2 / 8`. It models self-shadowing and masking of microfacets at grazing angles.
theory.energy_conservation: Diffuse and specular must not add more light than arrives. PBR solves this with `kD = (1 - F) * (1 - metallic)`: the fraction of light not reflected specularly goes to diffuse (Lambertian). Metals have no diffuse term (metallic=1 → kD=0).

challenges.title: Fresnel Term
challenges.description: Implement the Schlick Fresnel approximation and visualize it on the sphere. Use F0 = 0.04 (dielectric) and display the Fresnel value as a grayscale: white at grazing edges, dark in the center where the view is head-on.
hints: Schlick's formula: `F0 + (1.0 - F0) * pow(1.0 - cosTheta, 5.0)`. When cosTheta=1 (grazing), pow returns 0 and F = F0. Wait — that's backwards. When cosTheta=0 (grazing, H·V=0), pow(1,5)=1 and F = 1.0 (full reflection).
Clamp the cosTheta input: `clamp(1.0 - cosTheta, 0.0, 1.0)` to avoid NaN from floating-point imprecision. F0 = 0.04 means a non-metal reflects only 4% at normal incidence.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The Fresnel effect is why every surface — even matte concrete — looks shiny from a shallow angle. F0 is the only per-material Fresnel parameter in PBR: metals set it to their reflective color (~0.95 for gold/silver), non-metals set it to ~0.04. The `pow(1-cosTheta, 5)` falloff was chosen by Schlick to approximate the exact Fresnel equations with < 1% error for most materials.

hlsl.note: HLSL uses float3 throughout; pow() works identically.

challenges.title: GGX Normal Distribution Function
challenges.description: Implement the GGX NDF and use it to render a specular lobe on the sphere. Use roughness = 0.3 (moderately smooth). The NDF alone — divided by PI for scale — should produce a visible bright specular highlight that you can vary by changing roughness.
challenges.solution_note: Return alpha2 / (PI * denom * denom) from distributionGGX.
hints: The denominator is `denom = NdotH^2 * (alpha^2 - 1) + 1`. Square it: `denom * denom`. Divide `alpha^2` by `(PI * denom^2)`. This concentrates reflectance near the half-vector peak.
Use `3.14159265` for PI since GLSL ES 3.0 doesn't have a PI constant. The division by PI ensures the NDF integrates to 1 over the hemisphere.
challenges.unlocks_theory: GGX (also called Trowbridge-Reitz) became the industry standard NDF because it has a longer tail than Beckmann or Blinn-Phong specular — matching real measured materials better at high roughness. The `roughness^2` remapping (alpha = roughness^2) was introduced by Disney to make roughness feel perceptually linear: a value of 0.5 looks 'medium rough' to human eyes, not near-mirror.

challenges.title: Full Cook-Torrance PBR
challenges.description: Assemble the complete PBR BRDF using D (GGX NDF), F (Schlick Fresnel), and G (Smith GGX geometry). Apply it to the sphere with roughness = 0.4 and a gold metal color (metallic = 1, F0 = (1.0, 0.76, 0.33)). Add a Lambertian diffuse term for the non-metallic portion and an ambient base.
hints: The Cook-Torrance specular term: `(D * F * G) / (4.0 * NdotV * NdotL)`. Add a small epsilon in the denominator — `max(4.0 * NdotV * NdotL, 0.0001)` — to avoid division by zero at grazing angles.
Reinhard tone mapping `col / (col + 1.0)` and gamma correction `pow(col, 1/2.2)` are needed because PBR accumulates HDR values (>1.0) that can't be displayed directly.
challenges.unlocks_theory: This Cook-Torrance implementation is architecturally identical to what Unreal Engine 4's material system evaluates at runtime for every metallic/roughness material. The only differences in production are: image-based lighting (IBL) for the ambient term instead of a constant, a precomputed BRDF LUT for the specular integral, and optional clearcoat/anisotropy extensions. The core D*F*G / (4*NdotV*NdotL) formula is universal.

challenges.title: Roughness / Metallic Material Array
challenges.description: Render a 3x3 grid of spheres (using UV to derive which cell each fragment belongs to) with varying roughness (columns: 0.1, 0.4, 0.8) and varying metallic (rows: 0.0, 0.5, 1.0). Each cell shows the same PBR formula with a different parameter pair, creating a material matrix.
hints: `cell = floor(uv * 3.0)` gives (0,0) to (2,2) grid indices. Use `mix(minVal, maxVal, cell.x / 2.0)` to map column 0→min roughness, column 2→max roughness.

This creates a classic PBR material matrix: left column is very smooth, right column is rough; bottom row is dielectric, top row is metal. It's the visualization used in every PBR documentation and engine editor.
challenges.unlocks_theory: The roughness/metallic material matrix is the canonical PBR visualization used in every engine's material editor — Unreal, Unity, Godot, Blender. Seeing all 9 combinations simultaneously makes the parameters intuitive: rough + metal = brushed aluminum, smooth + metal = mirror chrome, rough + dielectric = matte plastic, smooth + dielectric = polished lacquer. You've now implemented the same model powering AAA games from scratch.
